All Questions
Were You Asked This?
Design a Web Crawler

Design a web crawler that takes seed URLs as input, discovers and crawls linked pages, extracts image URLs from web pages, and handles duplicate URL filtering while scaling to enterprise-level requirements.

Asked at:

ZoomInfo

Atlassian

Microsoft

Expedia

+3 More
Try This Problem Yourself

Practice with guided hints and real-time feedback

Start Practice

A web crawler is a distributed system that starts from seed URLs, fetches pages on the web, extracts links and media (like image src URLs), and continues exploring within a defined scope. Think of simplified versions of Googlebot or enterprise site scanners that inventory content and assets.

Interviewers ask this to see if you can orchestrate a high-throughput, fault-tolerant pipeline that behaves politely toward external sites, avoids duplication and crawler traps, and produces incremental, queryable results. The problem tests your ability to break a long-running workflow into resilient stages, apply backpressure, control concurrency at the right granularity (per domain), and make pragmatic storage and bandwidth trade-offs.

You should demonstrate a systematic approach: define scope and success metrics, design a crawl frontier and worker pool, enforce robots.txt and rate limits, deduplicate aggressively, and produce a mapping from page URLs to discovered image URLs with progress visibility.

Hello Interview Problem Breakdown
Web Crawler

System design answer key for designing a web crawler like Google, built by FAANG managers and staff engineers.

Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to submit a list of seed URLs to start a crawl.

Users should be able to retrieve, for each crawled page URL, the list of image URLs found on that page.

Users should be able to define crawl scope and politeness constraints such as max depth, domain allow/deny lists, and per-domain rate limits.

Users should be able to monitor crawl progress and fetch partial results via an API as pages are processed.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you enforce robots.txt and per-domain politeness across many workers?

How will you deduplicate and canonicalize URLs and content to avoid loops and wasted work?

How will you design the crawl frontier, retries, and backpressure so failures do not lose progress?

How will you keep storage and bandwidth under control while meeting throughput goals?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Multi-step Processes

Crawling is a pipeline—schedule URL → fetch → parse/extract → persist results → schedule children. Breaking this into durable stages lets you retry the failing step, scale each stage independently, and maintain idempotency across restarts.

Read More
Managing Long Running Tasks

Crawls run for hours or days and must survive restarts, partial failures, and pauses. Checkpointing progress, retries with backoff, DLQs, and idempotent writes are essential to avoid losing work or duplicating effort.

Read More
Dealing with Contention

The main contention hotspot is per-domain concurrency. Coordinated rate limiting and fair scheduling prevent oversubscribing a single site and avoid thundering herds across a shared frontier.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

Kafka

Kafka provides a scalable crawl frontier with high-throughput topics, partitioning by domain to cap per-site concurrency, and offset control for at-least-once processing. Separate topics for retries and DLQ make failure handling explicit.

Read More
Redis

Redis excels at fast, distributed coordination: caching robots.txt, implementing per-domain token buckets with atomic scripts, and hosting Bloom filters/sets for quick dedup pre-checks. Low latency keeps workers busy without overloading origins.

Read More
DynamoDB

DynamoDB offers scalable URL metadata storage with conditional writes for idempotent scheduling, secondary indexes for content-hash lookups, and TTL support to expire stale records—ideal for large, write-heavy crawls.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Job Scheduler

Both require durable scheduling of many small tasks, retries with backoff, idempotency, and progress tracking across distributed workers. The crawler’s frontier behaves like a large job queue with domain-aware constraints.

Read
Practice
Rate Limiter

Per-domain politeness mirrors multi-tenant rate limiting. You must coordinate tokens across workers, avoid hot keys, and adapt to backpressure and 429/503 signals to stay within site-specific budgets.

Read
Practice
Ad Click Aggregator

Both are high-throughput ingestion pipelines that normalize input, deduplicate, and persist summarized results. The crawler ingests pages instead of click events but faces similar throughput, durability, and fault tolerance challenges.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Ignoring robots.txt and per-domain rate limits

This risks IP bans, legal or policy violations, and unstable throughput as sites throttle you. A production-ready design must show concrete, distributed politeness controls.

No robust URL canonicalization and deduplication

Missing canonicalization and content hashing leads to infinite loops, exponential URL growth, and massive waste of bandwidth and storage. The system becomes non-viable at scale.

Putting large HTML payloads directly on the queue

Queues are for small messages. Large blobs increase latency and failure rates, complicate retries, and drive up costs. Use object storage for HTML and pass only references through the frontier.

ask this question

Design a service which receives as input a list of URLs ,scrapes those URLs for links to other pages and references to images(i.e., the src component of img tags), then returns a mapping of page URLs to a list of image URLs.
Your service does not need to download and store the images
Your service should follow links to other pages from the original submitted pages, and return the images on those 2/3/nth level pages as if they were on the first level page.

The API contract is defined as :
POST ing to /jobs with a body of a JSON array of URLs to start scraping from(e.g [“https:/google.com” ,https://www.statuspage.io]) should return a job identifier of some kind
GET ing/jobs/:job_id/status with the returned job identifier should return a JSON object of the format of {“completed”:x, “in_progress”: y} where x is the number of original URLs which have been completely crawled and y is the number of original URLs which are still being crawled.
GET ing /jobs/:job_id/results with the returned job identifier should return a JSON object returning a mapping of original URL to all reachable images from that original URL, in the format of
{
“https:/google/com”:{
https://google.com/images/logo_sm_2.gif,
https://google.com/images/warning.gif
],
https://www/statuspage.io: [
https://statuspage.io/logo.png,
https://statuspage.io/other-logo.png
]
}

I was asked to create the web crawler. Overall the standard design worked. There were lot of follow up questions on the URL Frontier Service and it's capabilities, data model for the URL Seed Store and how we will be able to fetching the next set of URL batch. Also there were follow up questions on the data store for storing the web page content. Overall on the same lines of the solution provided on HelloInterview but since I was done with the solution the follow up questions came.
